title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
NoteKey Ideas



By the end of this tutorial you should be able to:

Define classical measurement error
Describe how classical measurement error influences treatment, conditioning, and outcome variables differently
Apply instrumental variables and 2SLS to correct for measurement bias

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
1. Measurement Error: Definitions & Types
Measurement error is the misalignment between the true state of a variable and its reported state. We often assume that the variables we reason about are the variables we observe. This assumption rarely holds in practive. Instead, we observe some proxy variable i.e., a noisy or imperfect stand-in for the true variable of interest.
Understanding measurement error is important because the variable we draw conclusions with is not always the variable we draw conclusions about. To identify unbiased causal estimates, we need to understadn the relationship between the variable of interest and the noisy proxy.

1.1 Classical Measurement Error
Classical measurement error occurs when the observed variable equals the the true value plus some noise:
\[X^* = X + \varepsilon\]
where the error term \(\varepsilon\) satisfies four key assumptions:

\(\mathbb{E}(\varepsilon) = 0\) (zero mean)
\(\varepsilon \perp X\) (independent of the true value)
\(\varepsilon \perp Y\) (independent of the outcome)
\(\varepsilon \perp Z\) (independent of any covariate)

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
Question
What does the first assumption imply about the relationship between \(\mathbb{E}(\varepsilon \mid X)\) and \(\mathbb{E}(\varepsilon \mid X^*)\)?











X

X



Y

Y



X->Y





Ystar

Y*



Y->Ystar





UYstar

U_Y*



UYstar->Ystar








Figure 1: We observe Y* (the proxy) but want to reason about the latent Y. The error U_Y* enters only through Y* and is independent of X.





There are other types of measurmeemnt error (e.g., differential). We will focus on classicial measurment error throughout this tutorial.

2 Motivating Example: Subjective Well-Being
Researchers are studying the effect of happiness on physical activity. Because they cannot measure partipants’ happiness directly, they use a self-reported to determine happiness. The researchers believe that the self-reports, though not a perfect representation of happiness, correlate with partipants true happiness levels.

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
Note
Prior work shows that single scale surveys correlate with true metrics at roughly \(r = 0.5\), while multi-item scales reaches about \(r = 0.8\). Neither is perfect.

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
Other Examples of Measurement Error

Blood pressure recorded at a single clinic visit
Self-reported hours worked
Human error when reading time from a clock
Mechanical error from scales

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
3. Causal Estimand, Identification & Attenuation Bias

3.1 Causal Setup & Identification
Throughout the tutorial, we are interested in measuring the average treatment effect (ATE) of \(X\) on \(Y\). Most of our results assume a linear regression model. Below, we explain how measurement error in treatment, conditioning, and outcome variables can influence findings.


3.2 Classical ME in Treatment: Attenuation Bias
We want to determine \(\beta\) in \(Y = \beta X + \varepsilon\), but we observe only \(X^* = X + U\). Plugging the proxy into OLS gives:
\[\hat{\beta} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y,\, X^*)}{\text{Var}(X^*)}
= \frac{\text{Cov}(\beta X + \varepsilon,\; X + U)}{\text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(U)}
= \frac{\beta\,\text{Var}(X)}{\text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(U)}
= \beta\lambda\]
where \(\lambda = \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{\text{Var}(X) +
\text{Var}(U)}\) is the reliability ratio. In the derivation above, we leverage the classical measurement error assumptions that \(\text{Cov}(X, U) = 0\) and \(\text{Cov}(\varepsilon, U) = 0\).






NoteIn which direction is \(\hat{\beta}\) biased when there is classical measurement error in \(X\)?





Because \(\lambda < 1\), the OLS estimate attenuates toward zero. With \(\lambda =
0.5\), a true effect of \(\beta = 0.6\) appears as \(\hat{\beta} \approx 0.3\).









TipAttenuation Bias



Attenuation bias occurs when a regressor’s estimated coefficient is biased toward zero.


Explore the chart below to see how the reliability ratio affects our causal estimate. Notice that when the reliability ratio gets smaller, the fitted slope shrinks toward zero even as the true relationship stays fixed. In other words, the causal estimate attenuates.




                        
                                            


Figure 2: Attenuation bias as reliability decreases. The green line is the true slope; the red dashed line is what OLS recovers from the noisy proxy.






3.3 ME in Outcome Variables
We still want to determine \(\beta\) in \(Y = \beta X + \varepsilon\). This time, we observe a noisy outcome \(Y^* = Y + U\). Plugging the proxy into OLS gives:
\[Y^* = \beta X + (\varepsilon + U)\]
The measurement noise is absorbed entirely into the composite residual \(\varepsilon + U\). Because \(U\) is independent of \(X\), it does not bias the relationship between \(X\) and \(Y\). However, the residual variance increases from \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon\) to \(\sigma^2_\varepsilon + \sigma^2_U\), which inflates standard errors and reduces \(R^2\).


Exercise: Outcome measurement error
The following exersize shows how analysis with a noisy measure of \(Y\) compares to the ground truth. The cars dataset (built into base R) records stopping distance (\(Y\), in feet) for 50 cars traveling at various speeds (\(X\), in mph). Suppose a technician records stopping distance with a tape measure that has random error. We simulate what that technician would have recorded by creating a new variable where each datapoint is equal to the distance plus some random noise term.


Load the data in R

data(cars)
set.seed(5)
cars$dist_noisy <- cars$dist + rnorm(50, mean = 0, sd = 20)

Question 1. Compute the measurement error \(U = Y^* - Y\) (dist_noisy minus dist). What is the mean and standard deviation?
err <- cars$dist_noisy - cars$dist
mean(err)
sd(err)
Question 2. Check the correlation between the original dist and dist_noisy.
cor(cars$dist, cars$dist_noisy)
Question 3. Regress stopping distance on speed using the original dist and then using dist_noisy. Compare the slope coefficients, standard errors, and \(R^2\) across the two models.
summary(lm(dist       ~ speed, data = cars))
summary(lm(dist_noisy ~ speed, data = cars))






TipSolution





data(cars)
set.seed(5)
cars$dist_noisy <- cars$dist + rnorm(50, mean = 0, sd = 20)

# error stats
err <- cars$dist_noisy - cars$dist
mean(err)   # ≈ 0.0 ft  — mean-zero, satisfies classical ME
sd(err)     # ≈ 18.5 ft

# correlation
cor(cars$dist, cars$dist_noisy)   # ≈ 0.874

# regression
summary(lm(dist       ~ speed, data = cars))
# slope = 3.93,  SE = 0.42,  R² = 0.651

summary(lm(dist_noisy ~ speed, data = cars))
# slope = 4.02,  SE = 0.58,  R² = 0.499  




The two slopes are nearly identical (3.93 vs 4.02). The difference is due to sampling noise. The standard error grows from 0.42 to 0.58 (+38%) and \(R^2\) falls from 0.651 to 0.499. A noisier outcome variable means you need a larger sample to detect the same true effect.


Simulation
Each point below is one observation plotted as (true \(Y\), observed \(Y^*\)). Drag the slider to add noise to the outcome variable and notive how the statistics update: the mean stays near zero while the standard deviation grows.




                        
                                            


Figure 3: Classical ME in the outcome: true Y (x-axis) vs observed Y* (y-axis). The error mean stays ~0 while the SD grows with noise.

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
3.4 Measurement Error in Conditioning Variables
We still want to determine the ATE of \(X\) on \(Y\). This time, we assume there is some confounding variable \(Z\). Unfortunately, We only observe a noisy \(Z^* =
Z + U\) where \(U_Z\) is classical ME (mean zero, independent of \(X\), \(Y\), and \(Z\)).
Plugging the proxy into a multivariate OLS gives:
\[Y^* = \beta_Z X + + \beta_Z Z + \varepsilon \]
\(\beta_Z\) attenuates, but what happens to \(\beta_X\). We can read directly from the DAG and see that \(\beta_X\) is biased: there is an open backdoor back from \(X\) to \(Y\) through \(Z\), even after conditioning on \(Z\).











Z

Z



Zstar

Z*



Z->Zstar





X

X



Z->X





Y

Y



Z->Y





UZ

U_Z



UZ->Zstar





X->Y








Figure 4: ME in a conditioning variable. Z is the true (unobserved) confounder; Z* is the observed proxy. Conditioning on Z* instead of Z leaves the backdoor path X ← Z → Y partially open.






3.4.1 Bias in Multivariate OLS
Let’s explore how classical measurement error in a single variable affects coefficients in a multivariate OLS.
Remember that the variation in \(Z\) left after removing the component of \(Z\) explained by \(X\) is the residual variance from regressing \(Z\) on \(X\):
\[\sigma^2_{Z|X} = \text{Var}(Z) - \text{Var}(\hat{Z}_{|X}) \leq \sigma^2_Z\]
The attenuation factor in the multivariate model becomes
\[\lambda_1 = \frac{\sigma^2_{Z|X}}{\sigma^2_{Z|X} + \sigma^2_U}\]
and \[\hat\beta_{Z^*} = \lambda_1\,\beta_Z\]
Because \(\sigma^2_{Z|X} \leq \sigma^2_Z\) and \(Z\) is correlated with \(X\), we have \(\lambda_1 \leq \lambda\). In words, classicial measurement error in multivariate regression accentuates attenuation in the variable with measurement error.
With a single covariate \(Z\), let \(\delta\) be the slope from the regression of \(Z\) on \(X\) (i.e., \(\mathbb{E}[Z \mid X] = \delta_0 + \delta X\)). Then:
\[\hat\beta_{X} = \beta_X + (1 - \lambda_1)\,\beta_Z\,\delta\]
The bias term \((1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta\) is non-zero whenever \(X\) and \(Z\) are correlated (\(\delta \neq 0\)) and \(X\) has a true effect (\(\beta_X \neq 0\)). In other words, measurement error in a conditioning variables biases the treatment coefficient, and the direction of bias depends on \(\beta_Z\).
Explore the graph below to see how measurement error in a conditioning variable influence \(\beta_X\). The starting DGP is \(Y = \beta_X X + \beta_Z Z +
\varepsilon\) with \(\beta_X = 1\) and \(\beta_Z = 0.8\).
Try adjusting \(\beta_Z\) using the dropdown menu. What happens to \(\hat{\beta}_X\) when you flip the sign? In which direction is \(\hat{\beta}_X\) biased for different values of \(\beta_Z\)?






NoteAnswer





The bias on \(\hat{\beta}_X\) is \((1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta\), so the direction of bias depends on the sign of \(\beta_Z\) and \(\delta\).







                        
                                            


Figure 5: ME in the conditioning variable. Use the dropdown to change β_Z in the DGP; drag the slider to change the reliability of Z*. Green = true β_X = 1, red = OLS estimate, orange = theoretical prediction.

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
5. Instrumental Variables (IV) & 2SLS
Now you know that measurment error can cause problems… but what can you do about it?!

IV for ME in Treatment Variables
To recover \(\beta\) consistently despite noisy \(X^*\), we need a second proxy \(T\) for the true \(X\). The IV must satisfy three assumptions:

Exclusion: \(\text{Cov}(T, \varepsilon) = (\)T$ affects \(Y\) only through \(X\))
Independence: \(U_T \perp U_{X^*}\) (measurement errors in the two proxies are independent)
Relevance: \(\text{Cov}(T, X^*) \neq 0\) (\(T\) is correlated with the mismeasured treatment)

Remember that we want to measure \(\beta\) in We want to determine \(\beta\) in \(Y =
\beta X + \varepsilon\) and we measure a noisy \(X^* = X + U\). Then
\[\text{Cov}(Y, Z) = \text{Cov}(\beta X + \varepsilon, Z) = \beta \text{Cov}(X,
Z) \]
\[\text{Cov}(X^*, Z) = \text{Cov}(\beta X + U + \varepsilon, Z) =\text{Cov}(X,
Z) \]
so \(\hat{\beta} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y, T)}{\text{Cov}(X^*, T)}\). In practice, we can implement the estimator as a two-stage OLS:
Stage 1: regress \(X^*\) on \(T\):
\[\hat X^* = \hat\pi_0 + \hat\pi_1 T\]
Stage 2: regress \(Y\) on \(\hat X^*\):
\[Y = \alpha + \beta\,\hat X^* + \eta\]


Exercise: BMI and Blood Pressure
Below, you’ll see how instrumental variables can recover unbiased estimates.
Higher BMI is a well-established risk factor for hypertension. A researcher wants to quantify how much systolic blood pressure rises per unit increase in BMI. The researcher suspects that reported BMI is subject to ME due to scale calibration, rounding, or inconsistent self-reporting.
We treat the NHANES BMI column as ground truth and simulate two independent noisy readings:
\[\text{BMI}^*_1 = \text{BMI} + U_1, \quad U_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,4^2)\] \[\text{BMI}^*_2 = \text{BMI} + U_2, \quad U_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(1,\,10^2)\]
\(\text{BMI}^*_1\) is used as the mismeasured regressor; \(\text{BMI}^*_2\) is used as the instrument. The non-zero mean in \(U_2\) is harmless for IV because covariances are mean-centred — all that matters is that \(U_1 \perp U_2\).

Step 0: Load data and simulate measurement error
library(NHANES)
d <- na.omit(NHANES[, c("BPSysAve", "BMI")])

set.seed(9)
d$bmi_star <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 4)   # noisy regressor X*
d$iv       <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 1, sd = 10)  # instrument


Step 1: OLS benchmarks
# Benchmark: OLS on true (unobserved) BMI
ols_true <- lm(BPSysAve ~ BMI, data = d)

# What we observe in practice: OLS on the noisy proxy
ols_fit  <- lm(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star, data = d)

summary(ols_true)
summary(ols_fit)


Step 2: 2SLS with ivreg()
library(AER)
iv_fit <- ivreg(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star | iv, data = d)
summary(iv_fit, diagnostics = TRUE)


Step 3: Compare all three
models <- list(
  "OLS (true BMI)"  = ols_true,
  "OLS (noisy BMI)" = ols_fit,
  "2SLS (ivreg)"    = iv_fit
)

results <- sapply(models, function(m) {
  b  <- round(coef(m)[2], 3)        # slope (2nd coef after intercept)
  se <- round(sqrt(diag(vcov(m)))[2], 3)
  c(estimate = b, se = se)
})

print(results)






TipExpected results









OLS (true BMI)
OLS (noisy BMI)
2SLS (ivreg)




BMI coefficient
0.644
~0.499
~0.644


SE
(0.028)
(0.024)
(~0.043)



OLS on the noisy proxy attenuates the coefficient from 0.644 to ~0.499 (the reliability ratio \(\lambda = 7.4^2 / (7.4^2 + 4^2) \approx 0.77\) predicts \(0.77 \times 0.644 \approx 0.50\)). The 2SLS recovers the true slope. The larger SE relative to the OLS benchmark reflects the noisier instrument (\(\sigma_U = 10\)); the first-stage \(F\) remains well above 10, so the instrument is not weak.






4.2 Proxy Variables as for an Unobserved Confounders
Can we use instrumental variabels to recover unbaised effects when there is ME on a confounding variable?
Consider three variables, where \(Z\) is the latent (never-observed) confounder: \[
Y = c_0 X + c_2 Z + \varepsilon_Y, \qquad
X = c_1 Z + \varepsilon_X, \qquad
W = c_3 Z + \varepsilon_W
\]






Note





\(\text{Cov}(X, W)\): \[\text{Cov}(X, W) = \text{Cov}(c_1 Z + \varepsilon_X,\ c_3 Z + \varepsilon_W)
= c_1 c_3 \text{Var}(Z)\]
\(\text{Var}(X)\): \[\text{Var}(X) = \text{Var}(c_1 Z + \varepsilon_X) = c_1^2 \text{Var}(Z) +
\text{Var}(\varepsilon_X)\]
\(\text{Cov}(W, Y)\): \[\text{Cov}(W, Y) = \text{Cov}(c_3 Z + \varepsilon_W,\ c_0 X + c_2 Z +
\varepsilon_Y)\] \[= c_0 \text{Cov}(W, X) + c_2 c_3 \text{Var}(Z) = c_0 c_1 c_3 \text{Var}(Z)
+ c_2 c_3 \text{Var}(Z)\] \[= c_3(c_0 c_1 + c_2)\text{Var}(Z)\]
\(\text{Cov}(X, Y)\): \[\text{Cov}(X, Y) = \text{Cov}(c_1 Z + \varepsilon_X,\ c_0 X + c_2 Z +
\varepsilon_Y)\] \[= c_0 \text{Var}(X) + c_2 c_1 \text{Var}(Z)\] \[= c_0[c_1^2\text{Var}(Z) + \text{Var}(\varepsilon_X)] + c_1 c_2\text{Var}(Z)\]



If we express the structural parameters in terms of the variances and covariances of the observed variables, we get
\[
c_0 = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y) - \dfrac{\text{Cov}(X,W)\,\text{Cov}(W,Y)}{c_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}}{\text{Var}(X) - \dfrac{\text{Cov}^2(X,W)}{c_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}} \tag{10}
\]
but we don’t observe \(c_3^2 var(Z)\). If we have another proxy, or instrumental variable, for \(Z\), then we can rewrite \(c_0\) as
\[ \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)\text{Cov}(X,V) -
\text{Cov}(Y,W)\text{Cov}(W,V)}{\text{Cov}(X,V)\text{Var}(X) -
\text{Cov}(X,W)\text{Cov}(W,V)}\].

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
section: Now, only observed variables appear in the estimator.
text: Now, only observed variables appear in the estimator.

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
section: 6. Latent Variable Methods: SEM, Scoring & IRT
text: 6. Latent Variable Methods: SEM, Scoring & IRT

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
8. Takeaways

Measurement error is not a nuisance to note in a limitations section; it is a structural feature of the data-generating process with identifiable causal consequences.
Classical error in the treatment attenuates coefficients toward zero by the reliability ratio \(\lambda\). Classical error in the outcome inflates standard errors but leaves point estimates unbiased.
Error in a conditioning variable leaves an open backdoor path and can bias estimates in either direction.
Instrumental variables and SEM each offer consistent estimation under different assumptions. The choice depends on whether a valid second proxy or a full measurement model is available.
Before worrying about confounding or external validity, ask whether the measures themselves are valid proxies for the constructs of interest.

title: Measurement Error in Causal Inference
text:
